data.title: De-casinoization: possibly the only right solution for the future of the crypto industry
data.description:
Author: Jtsong.eth; Source: X,@Jtsong2

The crypto industry has been talking about Mass Adoption, but in reality: users have always remained transaction users, not "users." AI has the potential to help blockchain achieve true "value accumulation."

Introduction: When Users Change from "Humans" to "AI"

If we look at the crypto industry over the past decade honestly enough, we will find a simple but often overlooked fact: crypto products have essentially always been "serving human nature."

This is also the core driving force behind the successive bull markets in cryptocurrencies. And what is human nature?

  • Profit-seeking

  • Gambling mentality

  • Leverage preference

  • Short-term feedback dependence

Therefore, the entire industry has naturally evolved the most successful model: Issuing Assets + Trading + PVP (Game Theory)

From ICOs to DeFi Summers, to NFTs, and to Memecoin, they all essentially revolve around:

  • “Can I buy it earlier?”

  • “Can I sell it faster?”

Shift:

  • Determinism

  • Verifiability

  • Trustless Execution

These three points are precisely the "essential requirements" of AI systems.

Why is this crucial?

Because AI systems themselves have a fundamental problem: the **black box**. The fact that model decisions are not interpretable becomes a systemic risk when it comes to: unexplainable model decisions, unverifiable data sources, unauditable reasoning processes, and the fact that these issues may be tolerable in the Web2 world. Blockchain offers a solution: **bringing the key processes of AI "on-chain."** This is not just a "bonus," but rather the essential infrastructure for the large-scale deployment of AI. An important conclusion: When the primary users of the crypto industry are still humans: playing with human nature is correct. But when the primary users begin to become AI: playing with determinism is the only solution. This is precisely why the next generation of Mass Adoption in the crypto industry is essentially a process of "de-casinoization." The prosperity of the casino model is essentially a short-term, effective structural benefit. We must acknowledge that the casino model is successful. It possesses several extremely strong advantages: extremely strong user motivation (expectation of making money), extremely high liquidity turnover (high-frequency trading), and extremely low barrier to entry (buying long or short). However, these advantages are also its ceiling. What are the bottlenecks of the casino model? Over a longer period, the casino model will gradually reveal several unavoidable problems: 1. Unsustainable user growth (reliant on the "newbie" model) The casino model is essentially PVP, not PVE. In other words: The profits of one group must be built on the losses of another. This directly leads to: User growth being highly dependent on "new funds" Once new user growth slows down, the system begins to involute; Retention is extremely low, with the vast majority of users participating only once; Ultimately, this forms a typical structure: "Traffic-driven → Bubble expansion → Liquidity depletion → Price collapse". 2. Inability to accumulate long-term value (no "productivity") The casino model creates almost no real value, merely transferring value. In contrast: Web2 creates information, content, and network effects; AI creates productivity and automation capabilities. Most crypto applications are merely "more efficient speculative tools." This leads to a core problem: capital can come in, but it won't stay long. 3. Regulatory pressure is inherent. When the core activity of an industry is: High-frequency trading, leverage, speculation, it will naturally be classified as a financial risk system, not a technological innovation system. This is also why globally: Exchanges are subject to the strictest regulation; Token issuance is constantly under scrutiny; DeFi has always been in a gray area; The more successful the casino model, the stronger the regulation. 4. Product Experience Fails to Expand its Reach Most "casino-style products" suffer from one problem: Strong Financial Attributes > High Risk Perception > High Learning Cost. For Web2 users, there's no need to take on complexity and risk for the sake of "potentially making money." Therefore, while the crypto industry talks about Mass Adoption, users remain primarily trading users, not "users." The real breakthrough: Shifting from a "financial system" to a "productivity system" If the crypto industry in the past solved the problem of "how to trade value more efficiently," then the next stage must solve the problem of "how to create new value." And this answer may very well come from AI × Blockchain. AI is essentially a productivity tool, while blockchain is essentially: [Identification of rights] [Settlement] [Verifiability] The core significance of combining the two lies in enabling both the "production process" and the "production result" to be recorded, verified, and assetized. This is a completely different path from the casino model. A Key Paradigm Shift: From "Trading Assets" to "Generating Assets" Past: Tokens were financing tools. NFTs were collectibles. User behavior = transactions. Future: Data is an asset. Models are assets. The behavior of AI agents is also an asset. User behavior will shift to a cycle of creation → use → reuse → profit, rather than simply buying and selling. Case Study: How 0G Labs Turns "Memory" into Assets Taking 0G as an example, we can see a very different path. A core module of 0G is decentralized storage + AI computing, which brings a crucial capability: the on-chain realization of AI Memory. What does this mean? In traditional AI systems: User data is stored on the platform; AI's "memory" is not transferable; all value belongs to the platform. However, in an architecture like 0G: User interaction data can be recorded on the blockchain; AI's memory can become a verifiable, ownable asset; different applications can share the same "memory layer." This directly brings several changes: 1. Memory as Asset

Your:

  • Usage Habits

  • Preferences

  • Data Trajectory

No longer just "data used by the platform," but an asset you can own, authorize, and even monetize.

2. AI Agents Possess "Inheritable State" Traditional AI: Each application is an island. Context is not transferable. On-chain, however, AI Agents can share memories across applications. This means that: AI is no longer a one-off tool, but rather a "continuously growing digital entity." 3. New business models are emerging. When "memory" can be assetized: Data markets, Agent economies, and Personalized AI services will all become true PVE (Value Creation) systems, not PVP (Value Validation). Conclusion: The essence of Mass Adoption is "De-Casinoization". For the crypto industry to truly move to the next stage, it must undergo a fundamental transformation: from asset issuance + transaction-driven to productivity-driven + value creation. In other words: De-Casinoization. This doesn't mean trading will disappear, but rather: Trading is no longer the core focus; speculation is no longer the sole entry point; users are no longer just "gamblers," but "creators." The previous generation of the crypto industry focused on making money flow; the next generation will focus on creating value. And AI may be the most important variable on this path.

data.title: Young people from small towns who label large AI models
data.description:
Author: Sleepy.md

Datong, Shanxi, a city that once relied on coal for half of its economy, has now shaken off its coal dust, picked up a sharp pickaxe, and is smashing it down on another invisible mine.

In the office buildings of the Jinmao International Center in Pingcheng District, there are no more elevator shafts, no more coal trucks.

In the office buildings of the Jinmao International Center in Pingcheng District, there are no more elevator shafts, no more coal trucks.

Instead, thousands of tightly packed computer workstations have been replaced. The Shanghai Runxun Cloud Valley Big Data Smart Service Base occupies several floors, where thousands of young employees wearing headphones are staring at screens, clicking, dragging, and selecting. According to official data, as of November 2025, Datong City had 745,000 servers in operation, attracted 69 data labeling companies, created over 30,000 local jobs, and generated 750 million yuan in output value. In this digital mine, 94% of the workers are local residents. It's not just Datong. Among the first batch of data labeling bases designated by the National Data Administration, counties in central and western China, such as Yonghe County in Shanxi, Bijie in Guizhou, and Mengzi in Yunnan, are prominently featured. At the Yonghe County data labeling base, 80% of the employees are women. Most of them are stay-at-home mothers in rural areas, or young people who have returned to their hometowns because they couldn't find suitable jobs. A hundred years ago, the textile factories of Manchester, England, were crowded with farmers who had lost their land. Today, in front of computer screens in these remote county towns, sit young people who can't find their place in the real economy. They are engaged in a futuristic yet extremely primitive piecework, producing the data feed necessary for large models for artificial intelligence giants in Beijing, Shenzhen, and Silicon Valley. No one sees anything wrong with this. The essence of data annotation is teaching machines to recognize the world. Autonomous driving requires recognizing traffic lights and pedestrians, while large models need to distinguish between cats and dogs. Machines themselves lack common sense; humans must first draw a box on an image to show it "this is a pedestrian" before it can learn to recognize things on its own after absorbing millions of images. This job doesn't require a high level of education, only patience and a constantly clicking index finger. In the golden age of 2017, a simple 2D box could cost over ten cents, with some companies even offering as much as fifty cents. Fast-handed annotators could work over ten hours a day and earn five or six hundred yuan. In a county town, this was definitely a high-paying and respectable job. However, as large models evolved, the harsh reality of this production line began to emerge. By 2023, the unit price for simple image annotation had plummeted to 3 to 4 cents, a drop of over 90%. Even for more complex 3D point cloud maps—images composed of dense points that require magnification countless times to see their edges—annotators had to create a 3D bounding box with length, width, height, and tilt angles to perfectly enclose vehicles or pedestrians. Such a complex 3D bounding box only cost 5 cents. The direct consequence of the plummeting unit price is a dramatic increase in labor intensity. To cling to their meager monthly base salary of two to three thousand yuan, annotators must constantly and relentlessly improve their typing speed. This is by no means an easy white-collar job. In many annotation bases, management is suffocatingly strict; phone calls are not allowed during work hours, and mobile phones must be locked in storage compartments. The system precisely records each employee's mouse movement and dwell time. If a pause exceeds three minutes, a warning from the backend will lash out like a whip. Even more frustrating is the error tolerance. The industry standard is typically above 95%, with some companies even requiring 98%-99%. This means that if you draw 100 boxes and only two are wrong, the entire drawing will be sent back for revision. Dynamic drawings are frame-by-frame; vehicles changing lanes can be obscured, and annotators must rely on association to find them one by one. In 3D point cloud maps, any object with more than 10 points must be bounded. In a complex parking space project, lines that are too long or missing markings will always be found fault during quality control. Revising a drawing four or five times is commonplace. In the end, after spending an hour, you only get a few cents. A data annotator in Hunan posted her settlement statement on social media. After a day's work, she drew over 700 boxes, each worth 4 cents, for a total income of 30.2 yuan. This presents a starkly contrasting picture. On one hand, there are glamorous tech leaders at press conferences, discussing how AGI will liberate humanity; on the other hand, in county towns on the Loess Plateau and in the mountains of Southwest China, young people stare at screens for eight to ten hours a day, mechanically drawing thousands, tens of thousands of boxes, even dreaming of lane lines in the air. Someone once said that artificial intelligence appears as a luxury car speeding by, but if you open the door, you'll find a hundred people riding bicycles, gritting their teeth and pedaling furiously. Nobody sees anything wrong with that. The pieceworkers teaching machines "how to love" Once the bottleneck in image recognition was broken, large-scale models underwent a deeper evolution; they needed to learn to think, converse, and even demonstrate "empathy" like humans. This gave rise to the most crucial and expensive aspect of large-scale model training—RLHF (Reinforcement Learning Based on Human Feedback). In simple terms, it involves having real people rate the AI-generated answers, telling it which answers are better and more in line with human values ​​and emotional preferences. ChatGPT appears "human-like" because it has countless RLHF (Reference Language Learning and Highly Formal) annotators training it. On crowdsourcing platforms, these annotation tasks are often openly priced: 3 to 7 yuan per task. Annotators need to give highly subjective emotional scores to the AI's answers, judging whether the answer is "warm," "empathetic," and "considerate of the user's emotions." A low-level worker earning a monthly salary of two or three thousand, struggling in the mire of reality, and having no time to even care about their own emotions, is forced to act as an emotional mentor and value judge for AI within the system. They need to forcibly break down and quantify extremely complex and subtle human emotions such as warmth and empathy into cold scores of 1 to 5. If their scores don't match the system's standard answers, they're judged as having a low accuracy rate, resulting in a deduction from their already meager piece-rate wages. This is a form of cognitive vacuum. Humanity's complex and subtle emotions, morality, and compassion are being forcibly dragged into the funnel of algorithms. In the cold scale of quantification and standardization, they are drained of their last bit of warmth. While you marvel at the cyber behemoths on your screen that have learned to write poetry and compose music, to offer comfort, and even don a melancholic facade, outside the screen, those once vibrant humans are degenerating into emotionless scoring machines through day after day of mechanical judgment. This is the most hidden side of the entire industry chain, never appearing in any financing news or technology white papers. Nobody sees anything wrong with it.

985 Master's Degree and Small-Town Youth

The basic task of creating frameworks is being crushed by the conveyor belt of AI. This cyber assembly line is beginning to spread upwards, devouring higher-level intellectual labor.

The appetite of large models has changed. It is no longer satisfied with chewing up simple common sense; it needs to devour human professional knowledge and high-level logic.

A special type of part-time job is frequently appearing on major recruitment platforms, such as "Large Model Logical Reasoning Labeling" and "AI Humanities Trainer". The threshold for this part-time job is extremely high, often requiring "a master's degree or above from a 985/211 university", involving professional fields such as law, medicine, philosophy, and literature.

Many graduate students from prestigious universities are attracted to these large companies' outsourcing groups. However, they quickly discover that this is not an easy mental workout, but rather a form of mental torture. Before officially accepting orders, they must read dozens of pages of scoring dimensions and evaluation criteria documents and conduct two to three rounds of trial annotations. After meeting the standards, if their accuracy rate is below average in the official annotation process, they will be disqualified and kicked out of the group chat. What's most suffocating is that these standards aren't fixed at all. Faced with similar questions and answers, using the same way of thinking to score them can yield completely opposite results. It's like taking an exam that's never finished and has no standard answer. Unable to improve accuracy through self-effort or learning, you can only keep going in circles, wasting mental and physical energy. This is the new form of exploitation in the era of big models—class folding. Knowledge, once seen as the golden ladder to break down barriers and climb upwards, has now become digital fodder offered to algorithms, more complex to chew on. Faced with the absolute power of algorithms and systems, the ivory tower master's students from top universities and the small-town youths from the Loess Plateau meet with the most bizarre convergence. They all fell into this bottomless cyber mine, stripped of their halos, their differences erased, all reduced to cheap, replaceable gears on the tracks. The same is true abroad. In 2024, Apple directly cut a 121-person AI voice annotation team in San Diego. These employees were responsible for improving Siri's multilingual processing capabilities; they once thought they were on the periphery of a major company's core business, but instantly plunged into the abyss of unemployment. In the eyes of tech giants, whether it's a small-town auntie pulling boxes or a logic trainer graduated from a prestigious university, they are essentially replaceable "consumables." Nobody sees anything wrong with this. The Tower of Babel, worth trillions, is built with pennies of blood and sweat. According to data released by the China Academy of Information and Communications Technology (CAICT), the Chinese data labeling market reached 6.08 billion yuan in 2023 and is projected to reach 20-30 billion yuan in 2025. It is predicted that by 2030, the global data labeling and service market will surge to 117.1 billion yuan. Behind these figures lies the valuation frenzy of tech giants like OpenAI, Microsoft, and ByteDance, with valuations reaching hundreds of billions or even trillions of dollars. However, this immense wealth has not flowed to those who truly "feed" AI. China's data annotation industry exhibits a typical inverted pyramid outsourcing structure. At the top are the tech giants who tightly control the core algorithms; the second layer consists of large data service providers; the third layer comprises data annotation bases and small-to-medium-sized outsourcing companies scattered across the country; and at the bottom are the labor-intensive annotation workers paid by piecework. Each layer of outsourcing takes a cut. When a large company offers 50 cents per unit, after layers of skimming, the annotation worker in a county town might receive less than 5 cents. Former Greek Finance Minister Yannis Varoufakis, in his book *Technological Feudalism*, put forward a highly insightful view: today's tech giants are no longer capitalists in the traditional sense, but rather "cloud lords." What they possess are not factories and machines, but algorithms, platforms, and computing power—the digital territory of the cyber age. In this new feudal system, users are not consumers, but digital tenants. Every like, comment, and view we make on social media is providing data to the cloud lords for free. And those data labelers distributed in lower-tier markets are the lowest-level digital serfs in this system. They not only produce data, but also clean, classify, and score massive amounts of raw data, transforming it into high-quality feed that large models can digest. This is a covert cognitive land grab. Just as the Enclosure Movement in 19th-century England drove farmers into textile factories, today's AI wave is driving young people who cannot find their place in the real economy to the screen. AI hasn't bridged the class divide; instead, it's created a "data and sweatshop transport belt" stretching from county towns in central and western China to the headquarters of tech giants in Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou, and Shenzhen. The narrative of technological revolution is always grand and glamorous, but its underlying reality is always the large-scale consumption of cheap labor. Nobody sees anything wrong with this. The cruelest ending is fast approaching, and faster than ever. With the leap in the capabilities of large-scale models, labeling tasks that once required round-the-clock human labor are being taken over by AI itself. In April 2023, Li Xiang, founder of Li Auto, revealed data at a forum showing that in the past, Li Auto had to manually calibrate approximately 10 million frames of autonomous driving images annually, with outsourcing costs approaching 100 million yuan. However, after using large models for automated annotation, what used to take a year could be completed in approximately 3 hours. This efficiency is 1000 times that of humans, and this was back in 2023. Just last March, Li Auto also released its new generation MindVLA-o1 automatic annotation engine. A self-deprecating saying circulates in the industry: "The more intelligence, the more manual labor." But now, major companies' investment in data annotation outsourcing has seen a precipitous drop of 40%-50%. Those young people from small towns who spent countless days and nights sitting in front of their computers, their eyes bloodshot, fed a behemoth with their own hands. And now, this behemoth is turning around and smashing their rice bowls. Night falls, and the office buildings in Pingcheng District of Datong remain as white as day. Young people changing shifts silently exchange weary bodies in the elevators. In this folded space tightly bound by countless polygonal frames, no one cares about the epic leap the Transformer architecture across the ocean has achieved, nor does anyone understand the roar of computing power behind hundreds of billions of parameters. Their gaze was fixed solely on the red and green progress bar in the background, representing the "passing grade," calculating whether those few cents or dimes of piecework could piece together a decent life by the end of the month. On one side, the Nasdaq bell rang and tech media relentlessly reported on AGI's arrival; on the other, these digital serfs, feeding AI with their own flesh and blood, could only wait anxiously in their aching sleep for the beast they had personally nurtured to carelessly kick their rice bowls away on some seemingly ordinary morning. No one saw anything wrong with this.

data.title: Polymarket Ditches Risky USDC.e for Native Stablecoin in Its Biggest Upgrade Ever
data.description:
Polymarket, the largest fully onchain prediction market, is overhauling its exchange infrastructure, replacing bridged USDC.e with a native USDC-backed token, revamping its trading engine, and rolling out multisig wallet support—marking the platform’s boldest move since launch.

We've heard your feedback, and we're excited to announce Polymarket is getting a full exchange upgrade.

Over the next few weeks, we're rolling out a rebuilt trading engine, upgraded smart contracts, and a new collateral token (Polymarket USD) to move off USDC.e. 🧵

— Polymarket (@Polymarket) April 6, 2026

At the center of Polymarket’s upgrade: Polymarket USD, a 1:1 USDC-backed native token replacing USDC.e, the bridged Polygon version. By moving away from cross-chain collateral, Polymarket eliminates settlement vulnerabilities while keeping dollar parity.

Polymarket is Launching Their Own Collateral Token: Polymarket USD!@Polymarket just dropped a big update. They’re moving away from USDC.e and bringing in their own collateral token, Polymarket USD, backed 1:1 by USDC.

Rolling out over the next 2-3 weeks.

For most users,… pic.twitter.com/0GrJoXRGow

— Pranjal Bora 🧭 (@Crypto_Pranjal) April 6, 2026

“For most users, conversion is seamless with a one-time frontend approval,” Polymarket said, noting power users can manually wrap via the Collateral Onramp contract. This shift dramatically reduces counterparty risk, enhances transparency, and aligns with tightening U.S. regulatory standards.

Trading Engine Gets Full Rebuild

Polymarket is deploying version 2 exchange contracts, its most significant infrastructure change since 2020. With V2, Polymarket's faster and more efficient matching engine requires fewer operations and lower gas fees.

V2 will also feature an updated Central Limit Order Book (CLOB) combining onchain execution with offchain efficiency.

"During the upgrade, all existing order books will be cleared. There will be a short maintenance window. We'll announce the exact date and time at least one week in advance."

One of the most significant upgrades is Polymarket’s support for EIP-1271, enabling multisig and smart contract wallets to access institutional-grade trading tools that were previously restricted to traditional wallets.

EIP-1271 support signals institutional appetite.

Multisigs, DAOs, and fund vehicles use contract wallets.

Adding 1271 support means Polymarket is either seeing demand from or actively courting institutional/treasury participants who can't use EOA hot wallets for compliance… https://t.co/QMhFRHxHxN

— Arjun Kalsy (@ArjunKalsy) April 6, 2026

Developers integrating bots or custom SDKs will need to update orders, while most users experience minimal disruption during a brief maintenance window.

This technical overhaul coincides with Polymarket’s CFTC approval to operate an intermediated trading platform in the United States, enabling direct onboarding of brokers and customers through regulated venues. The move signals a compliance-first strategy, positioning Polymarket as the U.S.’s premier regulated prediction market.

Backing from NYSE parent Intercontinental Exchange ($600M investment) underscores institutional confidence and highlights the startup’s pivot toward mainstream adoption.

Explosive growth and institutional ambitions

Interest in prediction markets—covering politics, markets, and policy—has continue to grow, with March volumes hitting $10.4 billion, boosted by expanded trading fees.

V2’s developer-friendly contracts and native USDC make Polymarket an API layer for quant funds, automated strategies, and multisig wallets, previously excluded by technical limitations.

Polymarket’s bold upgrades trade bridged token risk for regulatory alignment, transforming the platform from a crypto-centric prediction market into a compliant, institutional-grade oracle infrastructure ready for 2026 and beyond.

data.title: South Korea Orders Monthly Audits And Real-Time Balance Checks For All Crypto Exchanges Following $56 Billion Bitcoin Error
data.description:
South Korea Orders Crypto Exchanges To Verify User Assets Every Five Minutes

South Korea’s financial authorities have imposed strict new rules on cryptocurrency exchanges, requiring all platforms to reconcile their user asset balances against actual holdings every five minutes.

The Financial Services Commission (FSC) announced the measures as part of a broader system improvement plan aimed at preventing operational failures that could undermine market trust.

S.Korea's FSC tightens crypto exchange oversight after Bithumb error. Exchanges must reconcile ledgers every 5 mins, disclose balances daily & undergo monthly audits by end of May. Stricter regs ahead. #crypto#S Korea #regulation

— Blockchain Report (@blockchain24R) April 6, 2026

Exchanges must also implement automatic trading halt mechanisms to stop transactions if discrepancies exceed set thresholds, alongside monthly external audits and expanded disclosure of holdings.

Why Did The Regulator Act So Quickly

The move follows a severe operational mishap at Bithumb in February, where the exchange mistakenly credited roughly 620,000 BTC, valued at around $56 billion at the time, to 249 users during a promotional event.

Each recipient received what was intended to be 2,000 Korean won but instead received thousands of dollars’ worth of Bitcoin.

Bithumb accidentally distributed 2,000 BTC to users instead of $1.50 rewards 🚨

Here’s what actually happened:

On February 6, 2026, around 10:30 UTC, Bithumb ran a “Random Box” promotional event.

The reward was supposed to be 2,000 KRW.

(Around $1.50)

Instead, an internal… pic.twitter.com/I3csOUUngv

— StarPlatinum (@StarPlatinum_) February 6, 2026

Some users sold the mistakenly issued coins immediately, causing a temporary 10–17% drop in the token’s price on the platform.

While Bithumb froze the affected accounts and recovered most of the assets, the incident exposed deep flaws in internal control systems across the sector.

What Exchanges Need To Change

The FSC’s new framework addresses these vulnerabilities directly.

All exchanges must now reconcile their internal ledgers with on-chain holdings at five-minute intervals, a dramatic increase from the daily or occasional checks previously in place.

Automatic alerts and kill switches must trigger immediately if mismatches surpass predefined limits.

Monthly independent audits replace the previous quarterly schedule, and public disclosures must now include detailed blockchain holdings for each type of virtual asset rather than a simple coverage ratio.

Manual, high-risk transactions such as event rewards must be executed from separate accounts, with validity-check systems that automatically block irregular inputs.

A third-party cross-verification is required before any payment is confirmed.

Most exchanges currently allow transactions to proceed with approval from a single manager, a practice the FSC has identified as a major risk.

Structural Weaknesses Revealed

Emergency inspections carried out after the Bithumb incident found that three of the five major exchanges only reconciled balances every 24 hours, while most lacked multi-step approval systems for high-risk transactions.

Proprietary and high-risk accounts were often combined, and automated verification of payment plans against actual recipients was missing.

Shin Jin-chang, Secretary General of the FSC, said during a meeting with exchange representatives and the Digital Asset Exchange Alliance (DAXA) on 6 April,

“The emergency inspection following the Bithumb Bitcoin mis-payment incident revealed not only the human error cited as the superficial cause but also structural and customary problems that have accumulated at the exchanges.”

🚨 CRYPTO: SOUTH KOREA ORDERS ALL EXCHANGES TO VERIFY HOLDINGS EVERY 5 MINUTES AFTER BITHUMB'S $56B BITCOIN BLUNDER

South Korea's Financial Services Commission is mandating that all crypto exchanges build always-on balance reconciliation systems operating on 5-minute cycles. The… pic.twitter.com/C5JIsNx7P6

— BSCN (@BSCNews) April 6, 2026

Regulatory Oversight And Industry Response

The FSC will strengthen internal controls to align with standards expected of traditional financial companies.

Compliance monitoring will move from annual to semi-annual inspections, with mandatory reporting to financial authorities.

Exchanges are also required to appoint dedicated risk management officers and form Risk Management Committees.

DAXA will finalise self-regulatory rule revisions by the end of April, with IT systems supporting constant balance reconciliation expected to be operational by May.

Key aspects of the reforms are expected to feed into South Korea’s forthcoming second-phase Digital Asset Act, giving regulators legal authority to impose business suspensions, personnel sanctions, and administrative fines for violations.

These measures aim to reduce operational risks and ensure that high-value transactions can be executed safely without relying solely on human oversight.

This regulatory overhaul represents one of the first cases where a major financial regulator has applied near real-time internal audit requirements directly to the cryptocurrency sector, targeting operational weaknesses rather than cyberattacks.

data.title: Who is the first US-based large-scale model stock?
data.description:
At an AI summit, Ultraman and Amodei refused to join hands

Author: Su Yang, Tencent Technology

The IPO race between OpenAI and Anthropic is one of the most watched IPO competitions in Silicon Valley.

The IPO race between OpenAI and Anthropic is one of the most watched IPO races in Silicon Valley.

Neither company wants to fall behind the other and both hope to complete their IPOs by the end of 2026. However, behind the competition for the title of "first large-scale model stock," their financial situations and internal pacing differ significantly. OpenAI CEO Altman wants to go public as soon as possible, but his CFO believes the company isn't ready yet. Anthropic is experiencing rapid revenue growth but also faces enormous computational cost pressures. Both companies rely on massive investments in computing power to maintain competitiveness, and the return on these investments is uncertain. OpenAI's Internal Disagreements Altman hopes OpenAI will go public as early as the fourth quarter of this year, but according to confidential financial documents OpenAI showed to investors before its latest funding round, the company expects to accumulate losses exceeding $200 billion before generating positive cash flow. A financial document shows that OpenAI expects computing power spending to reach $121 billion in 2028. Even if sales nearly double that year, the company still expects to lose $85 billion. Such a scale of loss is extremely rare among publicly traded companies. OpenAI Revenue Roadmap

OpenAI Revenue Roadmap

But CFO Sarah Friar disagrees with Altman; she doesn't believe the company will be ready to go public in 2026.

Sarah Friar's reasoning is that process and organizational work is not yet in place, and the risks of spending commitments are too great.

She was also unsure whether OpenAI would need to invest so much in acquiring AI servers over the next few years, and whether its slowing revenue growth could support these promises. Furthermore, Amazon and Nvidia currently hold substantial stakes in OpenAI, acting as strongly tied and strategically positioned "strategic shareholders," which could potentially influence the timing of its IPO. Regarding the disagreement between the CEO and CFO, Freer has deliberately downplayed it publicly, only emphasizing that an IPO is "not currently under consideration" because OpenAI is still striving to "get the company into a state of continuous upgrading commensurate with our current size." From their attitude towards the IPO, some subtle changes can be seen between Altman and Freer. In August 2025, Friel stopped reporting directly to Altman and instead reported to Fidji Simo, who joined OpenAI as head of its applications business. This arrangement is uncommon in large companies, where CFOs typically report directly to the CEO. Several people who worked with Friel told *The Information* that Altman excluded her from certain conversations related to the company's financial plans. For example, Friel was not present when Altman discussed server spending with the head of one of OpenAI's largest investors in recent months, even though she had participated in previous conversations on the same topic. Another person who attended an OpenAI high-level meeting earlier this year said that Friel's lack of invitation to the meeting, which involved significant financial decisions, was also unusual. It's worth noting that the concerns expressed privately by Friel are quite similar to recent public statements by Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei. Amodei said in a podcast this February, "Even if the technology really does develop at the fastest pace I predict, it's hard to say whether revenue will keep up. But the problem is, you buy data centers based on that pace (expected revenue). If your judgment is off by a year or two, it could be catastrophic." Amodei believes that even being wrong by just one year, or having a growth rate of five times instead of ten, could result in bankruptcy. He added, "I have a feeling that some companies haven't seriously considered the costs and benefits; they have no idea how much risk they're taking." Who are these "some companies"? Is Anthropic manipulating its financial statements? According to financial data obtained from the Wall Street Journal, Anthropic's revenue growth is stronger than OpenAI's. Its annualized revenue has exceeded $30 billion, and this figure is projected to be approximately $9 billion by the end of 2025. When Anthropic announced its Series G funding round in February, it stated that over 500 enterprise clients had annualized spending exceeding $1 million. Now, that number has surpassed 1,000. It doubled in less than two months. According to data compiled by The Wall Street Journal, even including training costs (bar chart), Anthropic will achieve profitability in 2028, while OpenAI will need to wait until 2030; however, excluding training costs (line chart), Anthropic will basically break even in 2024 and 2025. Analysts at Mizuho Financial Group estimate that Broadcom will earn $21 billion in AI revenue from Anthropic in 2026 and $42 billion in 2027. It should be noted that the two companies use different methods to calculate revenue, which results in OpenAI's revenue growth rate not being as rapid as Anthropic's. A key point is that Anthropic includes its technology sales through cloud partners in its revenue, while OpenAI's are not. This makes Anthropic's revenue figures look better. Anthropic responded that this is in accordance with standard accounting practice because the company is the main party in the transaction. Furthermore, despite verbally expressing concerns about revenue lag, Anthropic has never stopped investing in computing power. According to official information disclosed by Anthropic, it has signed new agreements with Google and Broadcom to acquire several gigawatts of next-generation TPU computing power, expected to go live starting in 2027. The vast majority of the new computing facilities will be located in the United States. Anthropic CFO Krishna Rao called this "the most important computing investment commitment to date." Inference costs are another heavy burden. While ChatGPT's revenue from consumer users is relatively large, paying users represent only a small portion; in other words, a significant portion of inference costs are not converted into revenue. Anthropic is in a slightly better position, with most of its revenue coming from enterprise clients. An OpenAI spokesperson stated that the company supports free users to promote technology adoption and can profit through advertising or converting subscribers. The spokesperson emphasized that the company prioritizes growth over profit. The pricing model dilemma: How large-scale model companies can price their products to avoid losses is an unresolved issue. Luo Fuli, head of Xiaomi's large-scale model division, recently analyzed this issue in a post. She believes that Claude Code's subscription system is ingeniously designed but may not be profitable, or even operating at a loss—unless Anthropic's API profit margin reaches 10 to 20 times, which she doubts. "In a single user query, some wrapper tools initiate multiple rounds of low-value tool calls, each round being an independent API request. Each request carries an extremely long context window, often exceeding 100,000 tokens. Even with cache hits, it's still wasteful," said Luo Fuli. According to Luo Fuli's calculations, the actual number of requests for each query is several times that of Claude Code's own framework. Converted to API pricing, the actual cost could be dozens of times the subscription price—a "huge pitfall." Luo Fuli said, "Large language model companies shouldn't blindly engage in price wars until they find a way to price reasonably without incurring losses." She believes that selling tokens at extremely low prices while simultaneously opening the door to third-party wrapping tools, while seemingly beneficial to users, is actually a trap. "Selling tokens extremely cheaply while simultaneously opening the floodgates to third-party harnesses seems appealing to users, but it's a trap. If users waste their attention on low-quality agent harnesses, unstable and slow inference services, and models downgraded for cost reduction, they ultimately achieve nothing—this is not a healthy cycle for user experience and retention." Conclusion: OpenAI and Anthropic are both vying for the title of "first large-scale model stock" in the US, both are involved in continuous fundraising and performance-based agreements, and both face the problem of continuously burning cash without fully validating their commercial returns. However, their situations differ significantly. OpenAI internally disagrees on the timing of its IPO, with Anthropic needing to control computing costs while simultaneously achieving rapid revenue growth. However, in terms of industry buzz, Anthropic's reputation is beginning to surpass OpenAI's. It can be said that in the exploration of large-scale models, no one can remain number one forever; a misstep in the technological path can lead to being overtaken by competitors. While OpenAI was the first company to launch the ChatBot AI assistant, it may not be able to maintain a leading position in all its businesses. In fact, from the perspective of industry health, given the continuously rising computing costs and the immature pricing model, how to build a sustainable business model may be more important than the title of "first stock." However, this assessment excludes those who simply tell the story.

data.title: Coinbase pushes x402 towards neutrality; Stripe bets on both sides outside of MPP.
data.description:
Author: Charlie Liu, Partner, Generative Ventures

Recently, more and more people are paying attention to Agentic Commerce, but the various protocols and players are becoming increasingly confusing.

V. This is not a simple protocol competition, but a layered stack competition

Once you understand this as a layered stack, many previously chaotic phenomena will immediately become clear.

The bottom layer is the paid access handshake.

This layer is concerned with: how the HTTP request expresses "payment is required here", and how the client returns the payment receipt after payment.

The main battleground between x402 and MPP lies here. MPP is attempting to bring 402 towards more formal HTTP authentication semantics; while x402 is more like platformizing 402, getting it running through custom headers, facilitators, on-chain settlement abstractions, and ecosystem integration. One is more like a standardized semantic approach, the other more like a platform distribution approach. Going up another level, there's the authority to spend, which is "who authorized this money." This layer is the key that many people haven't fully realized yet. Machines paying isn't that difficult; the real challenge is ensuring machines are trusted enough to make payments. AP2 is important because it's not just about "how to pay," but about addressing mandates, verifiable credentials, authenticity, and accountability. The agent tokens, instruction validation, passkeys, and dispute signals that Visa and Mastercard have recently emphasized are essentially all based on this. Moving up another layer, we come to workflow and distribution. These are things closer to "who controls traffic and transaction orchestration," such as discovery, checkout, merchant relationships, credential sharing, and AI surface integration. UCP and ACP seem to be vying for this layer. For B2B, this layer may not be as exciting in the short term, but its value could be very high in the long run. Because if more and more enterprise software is coordinated, invoked, procured, and paid for by agents in the future, then whoever masters the workflow language will not only manage a single payment, but the entire workflow. Once you separate these three layers, you'll discover a very simple fact: there's no need to expect a single protocol to cover all the problems. A more realistic path is for each of these three layers to grow independently first, and then gradually interlock through interoperability. And precisely because of this, investing in multiple options is not about wavering, but about rationality. VI. The Real Risk of x402 Licensing Is Not Regulation, But the Economics of Concurrency. If we only recognize "multiple protocols coexisting," we haven't grasped the full picture. The biggest risk of x402 is not necessarily regulation, but rather the time-of-check/time-of-use economics brought about by the two-step verify-settle process. Simply put, if verifying payment and final settlement are not the same thing, then in real-world internet environments with high concurrency, retries, proxy layers, and caching layers, the "pay once, access multiple times" scenario will occur. The x402 ecosystem is currently patching these vulnerabilities, such as with settlement cache, idempotency extension, and payment identifier, but this precisely demonstrates that the problem is not theoretical. Why is this particularly important for B2B readers? Because what the B2B world fears most is never the inability to create a beautiful demo, but rather having too many edge cases that eventually leak in production. API monetization, on the surface, seems like a small matter of paying a few cents per request; however, if your product charges per call, per result, or per workflow, then whether it's "pay once and get once" or "pay once and get many times" isn't just a product detail, but a matter of life and death. Therefore, if x402 redirects can truly succeed in B2B in the future, a crucial prerequisite isn't the narrative, but rather that these default-safe mechanisms must be made sufficiently straightforward; otherwise, companies won't feel comfortable connecting real traffic. VII. Protocols May Be Free, But Tollbooths Won't Disappear. There's another point I think deserves detailed explanation here. Many open protocols eventually reach a familiar point: the protocol itself becomes increasingly cheaper, even free, but real tollbooths will emerge alongside it. The same applies to x402. While standards themselves emphasize openness, neutrality, and zero fees built into the standard, this doesn't mean value capture will disappear. If x402 succeeds, the value won't primarily remain within the protocol itself, but will migrate to adjacent layers such as the facilitator, wallet and key management, discovery, policy engine, and trust wrapper. This is especially important for B2B companies. Because enterprise customers won't massively overhaul their entire system for a new protocol; what they're truly willing to pay for is someone who can help them manage the complexities of orchestration, policy, risk, compliance, audit, settlement, and permission boundaries in a multi-protocol environment. In other words, protocols will increasingly resemble low-level languages, but the layer that translates these languages ​​into capabilities that allow businesses to launch with confidence is more likely to become a new platform and a new tollbooth. This is why I believe that when looking at x402 today, we shouldn't just focus on who among Coinbase, Cloudflare, and Stripe is the "protagonist." What's truly worth watching is who has the best chance of standing on these adjacent layers. Cloudflare has its place in edge computing and traffic distribution, Stripe has its place in payment infrastructure and merchant relationships, Visa and Mastercard have their place in credentials, network tokens, and consumer trust, and Google has its place in workflow and discovery surface. True value capture doesn't necessarily happen in "who defined 402," but more likely in "who integrated 402 into a larger enterprise system." VIII. Conclusion The x402 Foundation is not an announcement that x402 has won over all agenda commerce protocols. It's an open admission that this generation of agent payments will never be a single-protocol world from day one. Coinbase handed x402 over to the Linux Foundation to make it more like a neutral public layer, rather than an exclusive product. Stripe is pushing MPP while simultaneously adding x402, not because it's wavering, but because it knows it shouldn't be betting on only one side. Cloudflare supports both because it's closest to real-world traffic. The actions of players like Google, Visa, Mastercard, and Adyen all point to the same thing: first, ensure interoperability between systems, then discuss who will ultimately dominate which layer. This judgment becomes even clearer if we shift our focus away from retail. Because the first to need these protocols aren't necessarily shopping carts, but rather the increasing number of B2B software and services that charge based on usage, tasks, and results. Retail is certainly larger, but B2B often reveals real needs earlier and defines what the infrastructure will ultimately look like sooner. In my previous article, I focused on interoperability, and I think the market's answer is quite clear now: yes, and even sooner than we thought. In this sense, the x402 Foundation isn't the end of the story. It simply shows us sooner that the real theme hasn't been "who will win," but rather "this world is destined to be interconnected first, and who can occupy the most valuable layer after interconnection."

data.title: Artemis: The Financialization of Uncertainty
data.description:
Author: Kaviish, Data Analyst at Artemis Analytics; Source: Artemis; Compiled by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Summary

  • Prediction markets transform uncertainty into tradable assets, whose prices reflect probabilities backed by capital, rather than mere opinions.

  • The industry is expanding rapidly, but current growth is primarily driven by sports betting, rather than by high-information-content markets that reflect their long-term value.

  • Encrypted channels break down geographical and settlement barriers, enabling global participation and enhancing signal effectiveness through scale.

  • Cryptocurrency channels break down geographical and settlement barriers, enabling global participation and improving signal effectiveness through scale.

  • The direction of regulation will determine whether liquidity converges into a deep global market or disperses into inefficient regional markets. The gap between the positioning (information infrastructure) of prediction markets and their current profit models is widening. Prediction markets reached $63.5 billion in trading volume in 2025. This figure is more than three times the volume processed the previous year. In the first 86 days of 2026 alone, the combined trading volume of the Kalshi and Polymarket platforms reached $52.7 billion, with Kalshi accounting for $28.3 billion and Polymarket for $24.3 billion (data from Artemis). On an annualized basis, the total size for the year will reach $223 billion. The industry tripled in size last year and is expected to triple again this year. Three years ago, prediction markets had a monthly trading volume of less than $100 million. Today, the same scale can be achieved in just hours. Federal Reserve decisions, elections, escalating geopolitical conflicts, corporate earnings results—any event can now be transformed into a market with a price. This price is not a simple prediction, but a probability backed by capital, constantly updated as new information flows in. Polls gather opinions, models process historical data, and analysts issue judgments, all suffering from the same structural flaw: incorrect predictions incur no cost, so accuracy is not essential. Prediction markets eliminate this flaw, giving predictions financial consequences: correct predictions result in profit, while incorrect predictions result in direct financial loss. The Federal Reserve has confirmed that prediction markets are more accurate and responsive than traditional methods. In a January 2026 research report, the Federal Reserve found that Kalshi prediction markets “significantly outperformed Bloomberg consensus expectations” in predicting overall CPI data. Since 2022, on the day of every Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting, the mode of the Kalshi market probability distribution has perfectly matched the final federal funds rate (Federal Reserve FEDS Working Paper 2026-010). Prediction markets have existed for decades. For example, the Iowa Electronic Market launched as early as 1988; another platform called Intrade also operated for many years before finally collapsing in 2013. Its core concept can be traced back to Friedrich Hayek, who proposed that the market is the most efficient mechanism for aggregating dispersed information. While this concept has always been highly persuasive, its implementation has been hampered by structural limitations: limited channel coverage, fragmented liquidity, slow and localized settlement. Today, all these problems are rapidly improving. Prediction markets are now directly embedded in brokerages, media platforms, and API interfaces. Robinhood has added event contracts to its stock and options offerings; media platforms like CNN are now adding probability markers next to news headlines, instantly translating each news cycle into tradable opportunities. Previously, trading based on macroeconomic predictions required opening an account, transferring funds, and waiting for trading sessions; these friction costs have largely disappeared. Prediction markets are becoming the default entry point for people to interact with and trade on uncertainty. Globalization of Settlement The second shift is even more crucial. Under the traditional financial system, prediction markets are constrained by geographical location, regulation, and settlement speed; however, these constraints disappear on crypto infrastructure. Users in New York, Lagos, or Jakarta can see the same market, the same prices, and the same opportunities, achieving near-instantaneous transaction settlement. Higher participation leads to greater accuracy in prediction markets. Confining markets to a single country naturally results in weaker signal quality; global expansion improves the reliability of the output. Cryptography not only makes prediction markets more efficient but also makes their price signals more authentic. Regulatory Game Regulation grants legitimacy to prediction markets but also exposes clear conflicts of economic interest. Prediction markets occupy a crossroads between finance and gambling, defying clear categorization and leading to inherent conflicts. This conflict is most evident in the sports sector. US states directly benefit from sports betting, a market projected to reach $165 billion in 2025, contributing billions of dollars in tax revenue (SportsHandle data). The existing system is built through state-by-state licensing, requiring operators like DraftKings and FanDuel to pay hefty fees, partner with casinos, and operate under high tax rates (Legal Sports Report data). While prediction markets fall under the same category as sports betting, they operate under a different set of rules. State governments view prediction markets as diverting business from tax-paying sports betting platforms, bypassing the long-established regulatory framework. This is why traditional betting platforms vehemently oppose them—not because prediction markets are flawed, but because they disrupt the established industry power structure and mature regulatory framework. Meanwhile, the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) is working to regulate prediction markets, while courts are defining the boundaries of their regulatory authority. This game will determine the final landscape of prediction markets. If forced into a state-based fragmented model, liquidity will tend to be dispersed; but if allowed to operate under a unified financial framework, liquidity will be concentrated, and prediction markets will grow into a global uncertainty pricing system. Currently, the vast majority of revenue from prediction markets comes from sports-related sectors. On the Kalshi platform, sports contracts account for approximately 83% of its total notional trading volume. Polymarket offers a more diversified range of contracts, but even so, sports contracts still dominate, accounting for 38.4% of its total notional trading volume. (Source: Artemis) This creates a significant mismatch. Current industry growth is driven by the sports market, leading to its widespread association with betting, a sector facing the most stringent regulatory pressures. As a result, the current revenue structure is closer to sports betting than financial infrastructure. This concentration of business brings timing risks. If sports-related regulations tighten before other categories have reached scale, the industry's core revenue source will shrink prematurely, while its long-term business model is not yet fully established. However, market valuations are clearly already pricing in its future potential. Kalshi is valued at $22 billion, and Polymarket's target valuation is in a similar range, meaning the market expects its size to far exceed sports betting, expanding into the institutional information market. This transformation has not yet truly occurred, creating a core contradiction: the prediction market is valued as information infrastructure, yet currently relies on sports-related products for monetization. The sports market contributes to growth, but it is not a long-term moat. Its true value lies elsewhere. The true core strengths of the prediction market: Besides sports, the prediction market has real and strong product value in several other areas. The 2024 US presidential election is strong evidence of this. Polymarket's presidential election winner prediction market saw a total trading volume of $3.7 billion. On the Monday before the election, Polymarket showed Trump with a 58% chance of winning and Harris with 42%, while traditional polls (CNN) showed a close race. Ultimately, the market prediction was accurate. A peer-reviewed study by the IMDEA Networks Institute in Madrid, Spain, analyzing 86 million bets, found that Polymarket's prediction accuracy exceeded 94% a full month before the results were determined. (arXiv:2603.03136) Prediction markets do not replace polls, but rather incorporate them into the system. Polls become the input variable, while prices, under the incentive mechanism of "loss for being wrong," aggregate all information to form the final judgment.

    Economics and Policy

    This is the most important track, and the real moat.

    Kalshi now provides intraday trading data for macroeconomic indicators, including CPI (month-on-month, year-on-year, calendar year), core CPI, PCE inflation, unemployment rate, non-farm payroll data, GDP growth rate, and recession probability.

    The Federal Reserve's research has validated the value of these products. In 2025, trading volume in economic markets surged by 905%, and in technology markets by a staggering 1637%. Although current trading volume is still relatively small, the signal quality has reached institutional level. The market's implied probability of interest rate hikes is not just a data point, but a key signal influencing the asset allocation, risk management, and strategic decisions of top financial institutions. It is here that the forecasting market is transforming from a consumer-grade product into financial infrastructure.

    Why Crypto Infrastructure is Crucial

    The key to moving from consumer products to financial infrastructure lies in scale, and scale depends on accessibility.

    Kalshi operates only within the United States, with a market population of approximately 330 million. Polymarket, on the other hand, is global, leveraging crypto infrastructure to allow participation from anyone with internet access and a wallet. This difference fundamentally changes market size.

    Users in New York, Lagos, or Jakarta can participate in the same market and trade at the same prices. Permissionless participation enables truly global reach. This is the channel advantage that crypto brings.

    Crypto also changes how markets are created: instead of relying on centralized operators, results are defined and settled by independent adjudicating bodies and oracle systems, allowing market size to far exceed the limits that a single platform can support.

    Crypto also changes how markets are created: instead of relying on centralized operators, results are defined and settled by independent adjudicating bodies and oracle systems, allowing market size to far exceed the limits that a single platform can support.

    Various trading platforms are converging on this model: Robinhood has integrated event contracts in addition to stocks, options, and crypto assets; around the same time, Hyperliquid also launched prediction market contracts through HIP-4. Market Overview Despite numerous market participants, the market structure has been a duopoly from the beginning. Kalshi and Polymarket together control 97.5% of the trading volume. The current industry shift is not one of further consolidation, but rather one of stratification: a clear hierarchical structure is forming around these two major platforms. The Disruption of Super Apps: Platforms like Robinhood and Coinbase are no longer merely distribution channels; they are directly penetrating the trading platform layer. Robinhood has already validated market demand. Prediction markets have become one of its fastest-growing products, with over 12 billion contracts traded in 2025 and over 1 million users. Its trading volume accounts for the vast majority of Kalshi's total trading volume, providing a clear incentive for Robinhood to internalize its traffic. In January 2026, Robinhood acquired MIAXdx, an exchange and clearinghouse licensed by the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), through a joint venture with Susquehanna. Coinbase is also adopting the same strategy. It first integrated the prediction market by connecting to Kalshi contracts, then acquired The Clearing Company to build its own infrastructure. Its strategy is straightforward: aggregate demand first, then vertically integrate. If successful, the existing market landscape will be rewritten. Kalshi's core positioning is at the licensed exchange level, but this advantage will be weakened if the distribution platform controls both users and trading venues. The industry moat will shift from licenses to distribution capabilities. Polymarket faces another constraint. After reaching a $1.4 million settlement with the CFTC in 2022, it withdrew from the US market and subsequently expanded globally based on its crypto infrastructure. When it returned to the US market through QCEX, it launched a completely different product: compared to its licensed global version, this version was more restrictive, required brokerage channels, and implemented full KYC (Know Your Customer) verification. Therefore, the product form that was highly successful in the international market is inconsistent with the version now competing in the US market, where domestic channels and funding channels are already controlled by existing players. This landscape will likely lead to a differentiation in roles: distribution platforms control user and order flow, exchanges provide infrastructure and compliance services, and native crypto platforms dominate global market access. Prediction markets will continue to grow at these three levels, but value capture will be concentrated in the环节 closest to users. Winners and Losers The emergence of this system has reshaped the winner hierarchy. Winners: Distribution Platforms Platforms like Robinhood and Coinbase directly reach end users, controlling account opening, funds, and order flow. As they vertically integrate exchange infrastructure, they will simultaneously reap the rewards of trading volume and profit margins. They are no longer just participants in the prediction market, but are becoming core entry points. Survival but Value Decline: Infrastructure Providers Kalshi has the best chance of becoming a regulated core pillar within the system, responsible for contract authorization, transaction clearing, and compliance infrastructure. However, its role will shift from directly facing users to providing services to user holders, with a business model closer to Visa than a retail bank. Maintaining Differentiation: Global Native Crypto Platform Polymarket retains its advantage in markets outside the US, where permissionless access, instant settlement, and broader market coverage are more important than regulatory compliance. It will become the default trading venue for global users, highly controversial markets, and crypto-native funds. Losers: Participants Lacking Channels and Liquidity As the probability of backed by funding replaces opinion-based predictions, the importance of traditional polling agencies continues to decline. Small prediction market platforms lacking liquidity and embedded channels will struggle to compete. Sports betting companies face structural pressures as prediction markets increasingly overlap with their core businesses, estimated to have resulted in approximately $600 million in tax losses. The Final Form: Prediction markets expand the pricing boundaries of financial markets. Today, financial markets price assets: stocks represent ownership of companies, bonds represent recourse to future cash flows. Prediction markets, however, price event outcomes. Everything uncertain can become a trading instrument: political events, economic indicators, technological advancements, environmental consequences. Ultimately, uncertainty itself is financialized. The data clearly illustrates the industry's development trajectory: 2024: Total transaction volume approximately $16 billion, the election validated the viability of the business model. 2025: Total transaction volume approximately $63.5 billion, a fourfold increase driven by the sports market. 2026: Annualized scale exceeding $200 billion, regulatory clarity will be crucial to the industry's survival (Data source: Artemis). Conclusion: Prediction markets mark a shift in how the world deals with uncertainty. It transforms the intangible probability of future events into a priced, tradable, and actionable asset. The core question is: is the essence of value in prediction markets (pricing uncertainty) aligned with the driving forces behind their scaling? Currently, the answer is no. The sports market drives scale, while information value gives it true meaning. The gap between the two will determine the future of the entire industry. Once scaled, it will not just be a new market, but a completely new model for pricing information itself.

data.title: Chinese President Xi Jinping Urges For The Massive Acceleration In Blockchain Development In The Country's Banking Sector
data.description:
China’s top tax and financial regulators are accelerating the integration of blockchain into banking infrastructure, urging lenders to overhaul credit systems and data sharing models—part of a broader strategy that also positions the digital yuan as a tool for global financial influence.

Blockchain-driven lending targets SME financing gap

China’s State Administration of Taxation and National Financial Regulatory Administration have jointly called on banks to embed blockchain and privacy computing into the country’s “bank-tax interaction” framework.

🇨🇳 China's tax authority urges banks to implement blockchain for lending services

China’s tax and financial regulators urged banks and local authorities to use blockchain and privacy computing to upgrade the “bank-tax interaction” model and expand financing for small businesses.… pic.twitter.com/DXXYIOEfe6

— QSI Media - News, Analytics, World. (@MediaQSI) April 6, 2026

The directive aims to standardize data sharing between tax authorities, financial institutions and enterprises, reducing information asymmetry and improving credit access.

The initiative is particularly focused on small and medium-sized enterprises, which often face financing constraints despite maintaining strong tax records. By enabling real-time verification of tax data, blockchain infrastructure is expected to streamline credit approvals, reduce fraud and improve lending efficiency.

Early implementations signal the direction of travel. Shenzhen’s blockchain-based electronic invoicing system has already demonstrated how distributed ledgers can enhance traceability, cut administrative delays and reduce errors in tax reporting—laying the groundwork for broader financial integration.

China's tech hub Shenzhen has issued over 10 million #blockchain-based invoices, as the city promotes e-billing, its tax service said Thursday. More than 7,600 companies have accessed the system to date, with an aggregate invoice amount of about $1 billion. pic.twitter.com/P6cwHDfcef

— China Business (@PDChinaBusiness) November 1, 2019

$58 billion infrastructure push anchors national strategy

The directive aligns with China’s long-term roadmap to build a nationwide blockchain data infrastructure by 2029. Authorities have outlined plans for annual investments of up to 400 billion yuan, underscoring the scale of Beijing’s commitment to embedding blockchain into the real economy.

This push builds on earlier policy signals, including President Xi Jinping’s 2019 endorsement of blockchain as a “breakthrough” technology for core innovation. Since then, China has steadily expanded enterprise and government use cases, positioning blockchain as a foundational layer for financial and data systems rather than a vehicle for speculative assets.

China unveiled a new chip that could make blockchain processing up to 50× faster.

An autonomous agent linked to Alibaba $BABA has reportedly decided to mine Bitcoin $BTC. pic.twitter.com/ThFMYYtHTL

— Rishith (@goswamirishith) March 10, 2026

Alongside its blockchain infrastructure ambitions, China is advancing the digital yuan as a strategic extension of its financial system. The state-backed digital currency is increasingly being used in cross-border transactions, particularly across parts of Asia and Africa, offering faster settlement, lower costs and enhanced traceability.

This dual-track approach—combining blockchain-enabled financial infrastructure with a sovereign digital currency—signals Beijing’s intent to reduce reliance on dollar-based systems and reshape global payment flows. However, the expansion has raised concerns among Western economies, where policymakers view the digital yuan as a potential tool for financial surveillance and a challenge to monetary sovereignty.

China’s embrace of blockchain however stand in contrast with its strict stance on cryptocurrencies. While trading and mining were formally banned in 2021, the country still accounts for a notable share of global Bitcoin hashrate, highlighting the complexity of enforcement.

💥 BREAKING:

🇨🇳 China announces a full-scale crackdown on crypto

Key points:
- No legal recognition of crypto as “money”
- Any crypto-related business classified as a financial crime
- Complete ban on foreign crypto services operating inside China

This is a hardline stance,… pic.twitter.com/vdB6QPx79M

— Crypto Tice (@CryptoTice_) February 8, 2026

The policy distinction remains clear: decentralized, speculative crypto activity is restricted, while permissioned blockchain infrastructure is actively promoted. In this framework, banks and state-linked institutions become the primary operators of blockchain networks, enabling controlled innovation within regulatory boundaries.

Beijing’s financial architecture shifts toward programmable trust

China’s latest directive reinforces a broader transformation of its financial architecture, where blockchain is used to embed trust directly into data flows and credit systems. By linking tax compliance with lending decisions, authorities are effectively turning verified data into collateral, reshaping how creditworthiness is assessed.

As investment accelerates and infrastructure matures, China’s model could influence how other economies approach blockchain adoption in regulated finance. Whether this framework becomes a global standard or remains a state-centric alternative will depend on how effectively Beijing balances innovation, control and international trust.

data.title: QuantumCTek, an AI-compliant asset, is undergoing a "revaluation."
data.description:
Author: Zhang Feng

Currently, artificial intelligence is integrating into social production and life with unprecedented depth, and its security and governance system constitutes the cornerstone of the digital age. However, a computing power revolution rooted in physical principles—quantum computing—is quietly approaching, and its potential disruptive power poses a serious challenge to existing security defenses and governance frameworks. Will quantum computing overturn the existing AI security and governance system? This is not only a technical issue, but also a global challenge concerning the future order of the digital society.When the leap in computing power encounters a lag in regulations, how can we prepare for "Q-Day"?

I. How does quantum computing threaten currently widely used asymmetric encryption algorithms?Asymmetric encryption The security of current AI systems, from model transmission and data storage to identity authentication, heavily relies on asymmetric encryption algorithms such as RSA and ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography). The security of these algorithms rests on the computational complexity of mathematical problems like large number factorization or discrete logarithms—problems that classical computers cannot solve within an acceptable timeframe. However, quantum computing brings about a fundamental paradigm shift. Quantum algorithms, exemplified by Shor's algorithm, can theoretically reduce the solution time of these problems from exponential to polynomial time. A paper review points out that the latest quantum algorithms, including the Regev algorithm and its extensions, are continuously optimizing their efficiency in breaking asymmetric cryptography. This means that once a sufficiently large-scale (typically referring to a general-purpose quantum computer with millions of stable qubits) quantum computer becomes available, the current "locks" protecting internet communications, digital signatures, and encrypted data could potentially be opened instantly. This threat is not far-fetched. Research from the Zhiyuan Community warns that this is an ongoing threat: attackers can begin intercepting and storing encrypted communication data (including AI training data, model parameters, etc.) now, waiting to decrypt it after quantum computers mature in the future. This "intercept first, decrypt later" strategy exposes all high-value information that needs to be kept confidential for a long time, including national secrets, commercial patents, and personal privacy data, to future risks. Therefore, the threat of quantum computing to asymmetric encryption is fundamental and systemic, directly shaking the foundation of the current AI and even the entire digital world security system. II. Facing Quantum Computing, What New Challenges Does AI Model Training and Data Privacy Protection Face? The development of AI relies on feeding massive amounts of data and training complex models, a process inherently fraught with privacy and security challenges. The intervention of quantum computing makes these challenges even more acute and complex. First, the long-term confidentiality of data throughout its lifecycle is compromised. As mentioned earlier, AI training datasets currently encrypted and stored in the cloud or during transmission may be completely exposed due to future quantum decryption. The Xi'an Jiaotong-Liverpool University's Global Anti-Quantum Migration Strategy White Paper explicitly points out that adversaries worldwide are systematically implementing this "data harvesting" strategy, patiently awaiting "Q-Day" (the day quantum computers become practical). This poses a fundamental threat to AI models trained on sensitive data such as medical records, financial information, and biometrics. Secondly, privacy-preserving computing technologies such as federated learning face new challenges. Federated learning protects raw data by training models locally and only interacting to update model parameters. However, the gradients or parameter update information of these interactions are themselves transmitted encrypted. If the underlying encryption is broken by quantum computing, attackers can reverse-engineer the original data characteristics of the participants, rendering the privacy protection mechanism ineffective. Finally, model theft and intellectual property protection become significantly more difficult. Well-trained AI models are core assets for enterprises. Currently, model weights and architectures are typically distributed and deployed using encryption. Quantum computing could render these protections ineffective, allowing models to be easily copied, reverse-engineered, or tampered with, leading to serious intellectual property infringements and security vulnerabilities. The China Academy of Information and Communications Technology (CAICT), in its "Blue Book on Artificial Intelligence Governance," emphasizes that AI governance needs to address risks such as technology misuse and data security, and quantum computing undoubtedly amplifies the destructive power of these risks. III. How Will the Development of Quantum Machine Learning Affect the AI ​​Security and Ethical Review Framework? The combination of quantum computing and AI—Quantum Machine Learning (QML)—foreshadows a new round of performance breakthroughs. However, it also brings unprecedented new security and ethical challenges, impacting existing review frameworks. On the security front, QML may give rise to more powerful attack tools. For example, quantum algorithms could significantly accelerate the generation of adversarial examples, creating more covert and destructive attacks, rapidly rendering current AI security defense systems based on classical computing (such as adversarial training and anomaly detection) obsolete. Some analysts have called "quantum + AI" the next battleground for cybersecurity, pointing out the need for proactive improvements to relevant regulatory frameworks. On the ethical front, the "black box" nature of QML may be more profound than that of classical AI. Its decision-making process, based on quantum superposition and entangled states, may be more difficult to explain, audit, and hold accountable. The ethical debates and risks surrounding QML, such as algorithmic fairness, liability definition, and technological controllability, have been extensively discussed. How can existing AI ethical principles (such as transparency, fairness, and accountability) be implemented at the quantum scale? How can regulatory agencies review a decision-making model based on quantum circuits that may exist in multiple superposition states?
